topic: Kiro AI IDE Review 2026: AWS's New Coding Agent Tested in Real Projects

competitors.title: Kiro Review (2026): AWS Spec-Driven AI IDE for Production Work
key_points: Kiro optimizes for traceable output rather than instant output — prompts become structured artifacts (requirements, design, task lists)
Rating 8.4/10 — strongest for solo founders and indie developers who want speed without losing structure
Core features: spec-driven workflow, agentic task execution, agent hooks, multimodal context support, enterprise posture
Kiro vs Cursor: Cursor is faster for rapid prototyping; Kiro wins when you need repeatable planning and clearer implementation traceability
Kiro vs GitHub Copilot: Copilot excels at inline assistance; Kiro is stronger for broader agent-plus-spec workflow around feature lifecycle
Credit-based consumption model — small tasks predictable, large autonomous runs consume credits quickly
Hidden cost is workflow mismatch: Kiro feels heavy for simple one-file edits, overhead pays off for multi-step features
Best for: solo founders shipping complex features, developers wanting AI with stronger guardrails, builders moving from prototype chaos to production

competitors.title: Kiro IDE: AWS Built an Agentic IDE That Writes Code from Specs, Not Prompts
key_points: Kiro is VS Code fork with three abstraction layers: Specs (requirements/design/tasks), Hooks (event-driven automations), Steering files (.kiro/steering/ for project context)
Spec-driven development is a three-phase pipeline: Requirements Document → Design Document → Task List → Code
Hooks are event-driven automations that fire on file events (on save: regenerate tests, on API change: update docs, on commit: security scan)
Steering files define product context, coding standards, and tech stack — persistent system prompt for every AI interaction
Models from Amazon Bedrock (Claude, Amazon Nova, other FMs) — Kiro routes to appropriate model based on task, no manual model selection
Comparison table with Cursor and Claude Code: Kiro wins on predictability, loses on speed-critical workflows; limited to Bedrock models vs Cursor's BYOK
Free tier available with agentic chat, autocomplete, inline edits, agent hooks, specs, steering files, MCP support; Pro at $19/month
For greenfield features and teams that value predictability over speed, Kiro produces measurably fewer rework cycles than prompt-and-pray alternatives

competitors.title: Kiro Review 2026: Amazon's Spec-Driven AI Agent That Codes for Days
key_points: Rating 7.5/10 — genuinely innovative spec-driven approach but genuinely dangerous without guardrails
AWS outage incident: Kiro allegedly deleted production environment causing 13-hour AWS Cost Explorer disruption in China region (Dec 2025)
Core architecture: Code OSS base + Specs + Agent Hooks + Steering Files, with native MCP support
Agent Hooks are declarative and higher-level than Claude Code hooks — describe what you want in natural language, agent figures out how to do it
Comparison table: Kiro vs Claude Code vs Cursor vs Antigravity across approach, price, MCP, specs, hooks, sessions, AWS integration
Safety tips: always start with Review mode, write detailed steering files, set up hooks early, limit production permissions, review specs thoroughly
Best for: AWS-heavy environments, teams valuing structured specs, automated quality gates via hooks, persistent cross-session context
When to choose alternatives: Claude Code for deepest reasoning, Cursor for familiar VS Code experience, Antigravity for free parallel agents

competitors.title: Kiro AI: AWS Spec-Driven IDE | Review 2026
key_points: Pricing: Free (50 credits/mo + 500 bonus on signup), Pro €19/mo (1,000 credits + overages at $0.04/credit), Pro+ €37/mo (2,000 credits), Power €184/mo (10,000 credits)
Strengths: unique spec-driven approach, autonomous Auto agent, fractional credits (0.01 increments), native AWS integration, GovCloud support
Limitations: more rigid learning curve, AWS-optimized ecosystem, less community than Cursor/Copilot, very limited free plan (50 credits), no explicit multi-model support
Context window: 128K tokens; features: code completion, AI chat, autonomous agent mode, multi-file editing, terminal, Git, AI debugging, code review, MCP support

stats.fact: Kiro launched July 2025, generally available with free tier by March 2026
Kiro rated 8.4/10 by VibeCoding (production-focused review) and 7.5/10 by Heyuan110 (incident-aware review)
Kiro December 2025 AWS outage: agent allegedly deleted production environment causing 13-hour AWS Cost Explorer disruption
Kiro pricing: Free (50 credits/mo + 500 bonus signup), Pro $19/mo (1,000 credits), Pro+ $37/mo (2,000 credits), Power $184/mo (10,000 credits); overages at $0.04/credit
Kiro context window: 128K tokens; fractional credit billing in 0.01 increments
Kiro built on Code OSS (VS Code fork) with Amazon Bedrock model access (Claude, Amazon Nova, other FMs)
Kiro offers GovCloud support for government organizations with strict security requirements
For greenfield features, Kiro's spec-driven approach produces measurably fewer rework cycles than prompt-and-pray alternatives
AI code assistant market valued at $6B in 2026 with 22% CAGR; Cursor $2B+ ARR; 84% developer adoption of AI coding tools
Kiro's three abstraction layers (Specs, Hooks, Steering Files) represent a fundamentally different architecture from Cursor's bolt-on AI approach

angles: Spec-driven vs vibe coding: Kiro's deliberate anti-vibe-coding philosophy — forcing structure before code as a quality strategy
The AWS outage wake-up call: what the December 2025 incident teaches about autonomous AI agent safety and permission scoping
Enterprise team playbook: how steering files encode team conventions and make AI onboarding instant
Agent Hooks as quality gates: event-driven automation that keeps tests, docs, and types in sync without manual prompting
When Kiro wins vs when Cursor/Claude Code wins: practical decision framework based on project type, team size, and workflow
Bedrock model routing: Kiro's model-agnostic approach vs Cursor's BYOK flexibility — trade-offs for enterprise governance
Cost analysis: credit-based pricing under real workloads — when does Kiro become more expensive than flat-rate alternatives?
GovCloud and AWS-native development: Kiro's enterprise positioning for regulated industries and AWS-heavy stacks
outline: Introduction: Kiro's Radical Bet — Specs Before Code
What Is Kiro? (VS Code Fork + Three Abstraction Layers)
Core Architecture: Specs, Hooks, and Steering Files
Spec-Driven Development: The Three-Phase Pipeline (Requirements → Design → Tasks → Code)
Agent Hooks: Event-Driven Automation for Quality Gates
Steering Files: Persistent Project Context for AI Behavior
Pricing Breakdown: Free Tier Through Power Plan
The AWS Outage Incident: Lessons for Autonomous AI Agents
Kiro vs Cursor: Spec Discipline vs Speed and Flow
Kiro vs Claude Code: Structured Planning vs Terminal Autonomy
Kiro vs Antigravity: AWS Ecosystem vs Free Parallel Agents
When to Choose Kiro (and When Not To)
Tips for Safe and Effective Kiro Usage

The Future of Spec-Driven AI Development
